steps.title: Support universal distinction
steps.content: The team's conclusion is that people across cultures can distinguish spontaneous laughter from volitional laughter. A finding from another study would strengthen that conclusion most directly if it identified a feature that separates the two types of laughter no matter what society the speakers come from. A consistent acoustic cue would explain why listeners from many cultures could make the distinction.

steps.title: Reject irrelevant findings
steps.content:
• Choice A concerns judging how well two people know each other, not distinguishing real laughter from forced laughter.
  • Choice C links community size with judgments, which suggests variation among societies rather than a universal ability.
  • Choice D emphasizes variation in spontaneous laughter, including across societies, instead of giving a reliable cue that separates spontaneous from volitional laughter.
The remaining option supplies a cross-cultural pitch difference between the two laugh types. The answer is B.
